item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article, across multiple sources, could not be sourced as being released under Creative Commons. Thus, it is to be assumed it's non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) and has been removed from the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No vote for now because there are some things I don't like about it. I like the idea but here are some problems I had with the execution.

Inside the casing of SCP-2297 rests a fleshy interior

I just don't like the idea that this SCP is biotech. I think the idea can do without it.

SCP-2297 determines for itself what is and what is not a room

I think this is redundant because if it didn't do that it wouldn't work. You could say SCP-2297 is mostly, but not completely consistent when it comes to what defines a 'room', which is closer to what you actually mean I think.

and will refuse to activate when placed outside.

I think the researcher is anthropomorphising it here by saying SCP-2297 actively refuses, as though it's making a decision. I think it would be better to say 'it doesn't activate when placed outside'.

All being said, it's your SCP and not mine so feel free to do what you want.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the concept of the SCP, but that last test log with all those redactions and expunging just feels cheap and not very well thought out. Do you know what's behind those bars and expunging? Because if not, and you just threw it in for the hell of it, then consider redoing that bit.

Otherwise, a good article, fun to read!

Neutral vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm no expert around this site, I haven't gotten my first official SCP down yet, but I really enjoyed this. The only thing I can say is the last log, as folks have said, has too much redacted. Maybe fix up a couple grammar spots and this could be sitting proudly with the likes of popular, well-known SCPs. For me, at the very least. ^^

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As far as I can tell, I'm not a co-author. I've made little to no changes to the anomalous natures of this SCP, and had no input whatsoever on the original drafting. I have, however, made many changes to the paragraphing, various sentence structures, and added some details - specifically to the addendum (in line with the original).

Thus, I believe I'm not self-upvoting when I +1.

item.title: Staff Post - Reversion Note (Closed)
item.content:encoded:
Following my correspondence with both DrJynx and BabaYaga, it is noted that the latter has been given permission by the former to make said content edits. To save us further trouble, DrJynx is free to keep the edits made by BabaYaga and can revert it in a manner suitable for them (DrJynx).

That said, it would be helpful for BabaYaga (or any other user given permission by the author to make content edit) to state (via comment) that they have been given permission by the author (re: non-collaboration articles).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The thermostat is implied as being a sentient organism, to some extent. It has no input. It just displays what it's about to do and then carries that out on its own volition.

For the most part, I can't help but agree for right now. There are some missing pieces preventing it from being "weaved together", as you put it, but I'm sure that there's more to come.

item.title: Staff Post - Reversion Note (Open)
item.content:encoded:
Noting that this article has been reverted to version 11 (last known version edited by the author DrJynx) as versions 12 onwards appear to be edited by user BabaYaga (some of which consists of changes in paragraphing). Please note that non-authors are not allowed to edit the content of articles they are not involved in. Instead, non-authors may only edit grammar, spelling and punctuation for non-collaboration articles they did not write.

BabaYaga, if you feel that your edits are justified, please go through to the author and let them make the change.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ultimately, I find that an object that affects an area in a direct manner of "Input fire and it will be hot" to be not worth much on its own.

As mentioned, the test logs are effectively an exercise of what readers can already expect and does not develop into more questions towards the SCP. If anything, your attempt at a finish resorts to a series of blackboxes that readers can simply choose not to care about. If they are censored and there are no hints to suggest they are worth additional thought, why should readers bother themselves with it?

Furthermore, the pieces that make up this article simply do not add up coherently. The thermostat that alters a room according to an input, the organic interior, the environmental conference… they are left in isolation to one another and not weaved together meaningfully to drive through one finality.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Fire is normal" on a control system like that is hilarious.

Of course, I thought it was a fire-suppression system, not a thermostat, which may explain something.

I guess my thought is I wish this was as funny as that initial impression.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not really more weird I'm suggesting. What it's missing is more along the lines of a hook or a twist: something to tie it all together, and or punctuate the story. (It's the most frustrating part of writing if you ask me- I've got a couple drafts lounging around looking for their version of the same thing.) Just thinking out loud here, but maybe something with the "[X] is normal" phrase… maybe something isn't normal?

item.title: Re: Chnages < misspelled
item.content:encoded: Thanks. It's much better now.

item.title: Chnages
item.content:encoded: Due to feedback I have entirely removed the recovery log, made the redacted section a bit more clear, done a bit of general article clean up and fixed phrasing in a few places.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the feedback! I've taken a look at the "XXXXXX IS NORMAL" test and tried my best to clear up the redaction a bit. I also have looked over the prose in my description but, likely because I wrote it, i'm having issues seeing where the problems lie. Is there any chance you could point those out to me?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First off thank you so much for the feedback, it really does help point out flaws that I wasnt able to see myself.

I've removed the exploration log and touched up things a bit, hopefully fixing all the issues others had with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey, thanks for the feedback! I struggled with the same writing it, but eventually decided that what it was would be weird enough.

Did you have something in mind? I'm more than open to suggestions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also neutral voting. Agree with previous critiques, but for me there's a larger-scope issue that I think would keep me from upvoting even if the rest were to be addressed: it never takes that one more step into the twilight zone. What I mean is that it never quite crosses from "thing that does something weird" into "thing which has ramifications I'll be thinking about all day" or "thing with a twist which legitimately surprised me and/or changed how I looked at it"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No vote for now. Some tonal issues that have been discussed above. And I agree ditching the recovery log.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pretty good, just delete the recover file and it will be good by me.

ok 1+

item.title: Ditch the recovery log
item.content:encoded:
Because it's daft.

That said, there could be scope for chills (NPI) if you had three test subjects and two step outside the room and number three sees them as they really are (burning to death), then realises he's burning to death too, he just doesn't feel it. That would be quite an "Oh bugger, I'm screwed" moment.

Anyway, ditch the recovery log. You know it makes sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Specific humidity is the ratio of water vapor to dry air, so a 200% specific humidity is possible (under laboratory conditions).

Admittedly, the more standard description of the condition is in grams of water per kilograms of air, so… it's not a great measure, but it is still something that can be represented by a percent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
OP thermometer isn't really doing much for me as a concept. But the bigger problem is the execution. The test log is an exercise in reinforcing a point that we're already aware of, over and over again. The over redaction and throwaway Voynich Manuscript reference made the last test especially silly, and it felt like an exercise in random spookiness for no real reason.

The recovery log doesn't make any sense to me. "MTF squad gets killed doing stuff" has been done dozens of times and is exceedingly predictable. But the way that the team is profoundly dumb about the nature of their work and the fact that they consist of Tough Military Guy caricatures (the repeated "lol hippies" stuff felt off) took this part from boring to actively bad.

There's also little details that are kind of off, but I'm on a phone and I can't line by line this. The two that stick out in my head are the tone breaking "meeting of minds at an environmental conference" and the bit about 200% specific humidity; I'm not a scientist, but I suspect humidity doesn't work like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm still against the recovery log. You didn't add anything to it to make it worthwhile, so this has to rely on the testing logs, which you also did not touch up or do much with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with much the above. Switch to Celsius for certain. Also:

A project to use more invasive forms of sampling is currently awaiting approval.

A think a better word than project would be proposal.

Lastly, I say drop the recovery log. It doesn't tell us anything that you haven't already told us, and thus is just a bunch of MTF swearing at each other. The whole thing is unnecessary.

I really like this one, but it needs to be touched up. I'll hold my +1 for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one has potential, but I don't think it's ready quite yet. Some of the prose in the Description is confusing, which should be cleared up. In particular, since the message is always "X IS NORMAL," that should be mentioned.

There were a few items I had issue with:

  • Temperature should be in Celsius, not Fahrenheit. Kelvin would also be acceptable, but not required.
  • Why "STARVATION" translates to low air pressure is not clear.
  • The action report doesn't really tell us anything new. It could also be formatted more in line with some other articles, with more spaces between speakers.

The "█████████ IS NORMAL" test is amusing, but the redaction could be handled better. I like the first section: "the coating of ████████████ that formed on the walls of the test chamber" is an example of good redaction. We know what's happening, but are left trying to fill in the specifics. However, "all D-Class underwent ██████ of a Type-██ class" is less good, because we aren't given a hint as to what the problem was, or why, or why the Voynich Manuscript matters. In the first case, we learn enough to let our imaginations run wild. In the second case, we are simply left bewildered.

Finally, the repeating of some tests under these conditions with more D-class isn't really what we would do anymore. Once we know if/how it's fatal, we aren't going to sacrifice other people to it in the name of reproducibility.

All in all, the idea is solid, and the results are interesting, but there are still too many loose ends and a clearer understanding of what's going on would serve this piece much better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My first "real" attempt at writing up a full SCP. Many thanks to uses of the chat and forums as well as reddit users DankidyDan2521 and HoneyBadger154 for helping me fine tune this.
